name: Bil
shortDescription: Bil is a child in Norse mythology who, along with her brother Hjúki, follows the moon god Máni.
description: Bil is a figure from Norse mythology, primarily known through references in the Prose Edda of Snorri Sturluson. She is depicted as a child, specifically the sister of Hjúki, and both are associated with the moon god, Máni. According to Snorri, Máni took Bil and Hjúki from Earth while they were fetching water from a well called Byrgir, carrying the bucket Sägr and the pole Simul. Their purpose is to follow Máni, although the exact nature of their role is not explicitly detailed in surviving texts. The limited information makes her significance somewhat enigmatic, but she is generally interpreted as a personification of lunar features or phases. Bil's story highlights the Norse fascination with celestial bodies and their attempts to explain natural phenomena through mythology.
subtitle: Moon's Companion, Child of the Heavens
realm: The heavens, specifically following the moon
role: Companion of the moon god Máni

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The primary source for Bil is the Prose Edda, specifically the Gylfaginning section. Snorri Sturluson mentions Bil and her brother Hjúki being taken from Earth by Máni while they were fetching water. They are described as carrying a bucket (Sägr) and a pole (Simul). The exact circumstances and reasons for their abduction are not fully explained, leaving room for interpretation. Some scholars suggest that the story could be related to observations of the moon's phases, with Bil and Hjúki perhaps representing lunar features or the movement of the moon across the sky. The narrative is brief, but it provides a glimpse into the Norse understanding of celestial mechanics intertwined with mythological storytelling. The lack of further elaboration in other sources makes it challenging to reconstruct a more complete picture of Bil's role.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Bil's role within Norse mythology is somewhat ambiguous due to the limited information available. She is primarily significant as a companion to the moon god Máni. Her presence suggests a connection between the earthly realm and the celestial sphere, implying that even children could be drawn into the workings of the cosmos. Some interpretations propose that Bil and Hjúki represent the waxing and waning phases of the moon, or possibly lunar craters. Others suggest a connection to the cyclical nature of time. While her cultic significance is unknown, she underscores the Norse people's keen observation of the natural world and their efforts to explain it through myth and allegory. Her association with water fetching may also point to a connection with the tides and the moon's influence on them.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Bil's symbolism is closely tied to the moon and its cycles. Her name is thought to be related to the Old Norse word 'bild,' meaning 'a moment' or 'split,' which could allude to the phases of the moon as it waxes and wanes. The act of fetching water with a bucket and pole might symbolize the cyclical nature of water, perhaps representing the tides or the flow of time itself. The abduction by Máni suggests a divine intervention, highlighting the power of the celestial bodies over earthly affairs. The interpretation of Bil as a personification of lunar features is a common one, offering a tangible explanation for the patterns observed in the night sky. The fact that she is a child further emphasizes the idea of innocence and wonder in the face of the vast cosmos.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Due to the limited information about Bil in the primary sources, her direct legacy and influence on modern culture are relatively small. However, she has found some resonance in contemporary paganism and Norse mythology enthusiasts. Her name and story are occasionally referenced in modern literature, art, and music inspired by Norse mythology. She serves as a reminder of the Norse people's deep connection to the natural world and their attempts to understand the cosmos through mythological narratives. The figure of Bil, though minor, contributes to the rich tapestry of Norse mythology and continues to inspire creative interpretations and explorations of ancient beliefs. Her story is a testament to the enduring power of myth to capture the imagination and offer insights into the human condition.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: The primary source mentioning Bil and her association with the moon god Máni.

sources.author: Rudolf Simek
sources.description: A comprehensive reference work on Norse mythology, providing interpretations of various figures and concepts.

companions.name: Máni
companions.description: The moon god who took Bil and Hjúki from Earth.

cultural.modernLegacy: Bil is a minor figure in modern Norse paganism, sometimes invoked in lunar rituals or considered a representation of childhood wonder and connection to nature. She appears in some modern fiction and art inspired by Norse mythology.
